comprehensive_summary:
Bertolt Brecht was a German playwright and the leading practitioner of 'epic theatre,' a theatrical paradigm he developed in opposition to Aristotelian drama. Central to his theory is the 'alienation effect' (also called the 'distancing effect' or 'estrangement effect'), which he coined after studying Chinese theater and the performances of Mei Lanfang. He elaborated this concept in the essay 'On Chinese Acting' (also called 'Alienation Effects in Chinese Acting'), noting how Chinese theater's lack of a fourth wall and strict conventionality created the effect he sought. He described the gestus technique, where characters embody social commentary through gestures and express attitudes physically. In 'A Short Organum for the Theatre,' he described epic theatre's principles. His 'non-Aristotelian drama' eliminated catharsis and the purging of emotions through empathy, creating 'untragic heroes' instead. Frederic Jameson noted that Brecht stripped back the 'well-made production' by forcing actors to question their decisions through verbalizing alternative choices. Walter Benjamin wrote the essay 'What is Epic Theater?' discussing Brecht's theories. Roland Barthes contrasted epic theatre with the 'Zhdanovian impasse' of propaganda.

Brecht's most frequently clued work is Mother Courage and Her Children, set during the Thirty Years' War. The title character, Anna Fierling, sells mouldy bread to soldiers and loses her children Kattrin, Eilif, and Swiss Cheese during the war. Helene Weigel, Brecht's second wife, originated the title role and delivered a famous 'silent scream' accompanied by increasingly loud bass and treble pitch after refusing to acknowledge the body of Swiss Cheese. The play ends with Mother Courage singing a lullaby and hitching herself to a wagon. The prostitute Yvette sings the 'Fraternization Song' in this play. George Steiner praised Weigel's performance for 'making the audience bow their heads as if they had been hit by a blast of wind.' Margarete Steffin was a collaborator on this play.

The Threepenny Opera, co-written with Kurt Weill and Elisabeth Hauptmann, is based on John Gay's The Beggar's Opera. It features 'Mack the Knife' (about 'old Macheath' who wears 'fancy gloves' and keeps a 'jack-knife' 'out of sight') and 'Pirate Jenny,' whose singer is bribed by Polly Peachum to turn over Polly's husband. Brecht fought bitterly with director G.W. Pabst over the screenplay of a 1931 film adaptation starring Rudolf Forster and Lotte Lenya. The Threepenny Novel features the one-legged George Fewkoombey, who murders William Coax with his own prosthetic leg before being falsely convicted and hanged for the drowning of Mary Sawyer. Elisabeth Hauptmann is sometimes purported to have written the lion's share of The Threepenny Opera.

The Caucasian Chalk Circle was inspired by a Chinese zaju gong'an drama by Li Xingdao in which Judge Bao Zheng identifies a child's mother using a chalk circle. Brecht adapted this into a play-within-a-play set during a conflict between two communes. The maid Grusha refuses to pull the child Michael out of the title chalk circle, and the judge Azdak makes a Solomonic ruling in her favor over Natella. A Singer performs works about 'The Four Generals' and 'The Rotten Bridge.' An earlier version, 'The Augsburg Chalk Circle,' is a short story set in Augsburg where Frau Zingli spends so much time packing her clothes she abandons her son. The play ends by declaring 'There must be happy endings, must, must, must!' and at one point an actor laments 'There must, there must, be some end that would fit.' Athol Fugard's Circle Players were named for this play.

The Good Person of Szechwan features Shen Teh, who is given funds by three gods to buy a tobacco shop. She puts on a mask and impersonates her male cousin Shui Ta, singing in a man's voice 'without trampling down twelve others / You cannot help one poor man.' A water-carrier goes to Shui Ta to get lodging for some gods. At the end, three gods sing, smile, and wave as they float away on a cloud, ignoring cries for help. It was adapted in Ghana by Mohammed ben Abdallah as Land of a Million Magicians, and John Ruganda translated it into Swahili.

The Resistible Rise of Arturo Ui is a 'parable play' allegorizing the rise of the Nazis, set among Chicago gangsters expanding their corrupt cauliflower empire into Cicero, Illinois. The central character seduces Ignatius Dullfeet's wife Betty in a scene paralleling Richard III. The protagonist frames Fish for a warehouse fire while working with the Cauliflower Trust. The epilogue declares 'don't rejoice too soon at your escape- / The womb he crawled from still is going strong.'

Saint Joan of the Stockyards features the title character as a lieutenant in a group called Black Straw Hats who organizes with meat packers against their boss, the meatpacking tycoon Pierpont Mauler, who becomes disaffected with capitalism after meeting her. A song declares 'you folk that are poor / are an army vast and grand' sung to seventy-thousand workers outside the Lennox packing plant.

Life of Galileo was revised in collaboration with actor Charles Laughton in the months after the atomic bombing of Japan. The title character's daughter Virginia dies of dysentery in a convent after being unable to find a husband. The play ends with a copy of the Discorsi being smuggled out of Florence by Andrea Sarti.

Brecht also developed 'learning-plays' (Lehrstucke), including The Baden-Baden Lesson on Consent, which caused a riot after a scene where the clown Smith is dismembered by friends to ease his pain, and The Measures Taken, where four Communist agents explain their decision to murder a comrade during a Chinese revolution. In the Jungle of Cities is set in 1912 Chicago and depicts Shlink and Garga engaging in a metaphorical boxing match. Mr Puntila and His Man Matti, set in Finland, was developed with Margarete Steffin from a translation of the Finnish play The Sawdust Princess; the chauffeur Matti holds up a landowner's plan to marry his daughter Eva to a diplomat.

Brecht's poetry includes 'Alabama Song' ('show me the way / to the next whiskey bar,' later recorded by The Doors), 'Reminiscence of Marie A.' (about a kiss under a plum tree while a white cloud passed overhead), 'Children's Hymn' (a response to Germany's national anthem), and 'How Fortunate the Man with None' (observing that 'sagacious Solomon,' 'courageous Caesar,' and 'honest Socrates' all met ignominious fates). He also suggested the idea for The Cradle Will Rock by Marc Blitzstein and was its dedicatee. His only Hollywood screenplay was Hangmen Also Die, directed by a fellow German expat. He wrote the slapstick film Mysteries of a Barbershop starring Karl Valentin, a main influence on his work. The minimalist staging of Lars von Trier's Dogville was influenced by Brecht. Brecht's work has been widely adapted in Africa, with Rose Mbowa creating Mother Uganda and Her Children, Wole Soyinka adapting The Threepenny Opera as Opera Wonyosi, and Augusto Boal developing the Theater of the Oppressed as an alternative to Brecht's didacticism.
discarded_note: Discarded 13 tossups about Albrecht Durer, Edmund Husserl, Wallenstein/Schiller, Hamlet, Walter Benjamin's essay, and shared-first-name questions. Discarded 12 bonuses about Durer, Husserl, Schiller, barnstorming, Northern Renaissance donors, photodisintegration, and gender theory.

works.name: Mother Courage and Her Children
works.description: Brecht's most frequently clued work, set during the Thirty Years' War. The title character Anna Fierling sells goods to soldiers and progressively loses all three of her children. Helene Weigel's landmark performance is itself a frequent clue target.

clues.clue: Set during the Thirty Years' War; the title character Anna Fierling sells mouldy bread to soldiers and loses her children.
quotes: This author also created Anna Fierling, who sells mouldy bread to soldiers during the Thirty Years' War.
this dramatist popularized the 'alienation effect,' exemplified by the stark scenery of a play in which Anna Fierling loses her children during the Thirty Years' War

clues.clue: Mother Courage's children are named Kattrin, Eilif, and Swiss Cheese.
quotes: Brecht's second wife, Helene Weigel, originated this title role, who loses her kids Kattrin, Eilif, and Swiss Cheese during the Thirty Years War.

clues.clue: Helene Weigel originated the title role and performed a famous 'silent scream' after refusing to acknowledge the body of her son Swiss Cheese.
quotes: An increasingly loud bass and treble pitch accompanied a 'silent scream' performed by Helene Weigel in a play by this author.
George Steiner praised Helene Weigel's performance of this role for 'making the audience bow their heads as if they had been hit by a blast of wind.'

clues.clue: The prostitute Yvette sings the 'Fraternization Song.'
quotes: The prostitute Yvette sings the 'Fraternization Song' in a play by this author
This author of Saint Joan of the Stockyards wrote a play where the prostitute Yvette sings a song of fraternization.

clues.clue: The play ends with the protagonist singing a lullaby and hitching herself to a wagon.
quotes: a play by this author that ends with the protagonist singing a lullaby and hitching herself to a wagon

clues.clue: Rose Mbowa adapted it as Mother Uganda and Her Children; the first authorized production in an African language starred Mbowa.
quotes: The first authorized production of a play by this author in an African language starred Rose Mbowa, who wrote a similarly titled play about a personified Uganda.
Rose Mbowa's Mother Uganda and Her Children alters the title of one of this author's plays.

clues.clue: Margarete Steffin was a collaborator on this play.
quotes: Mother Courage and her Children was partly written by Margarete Steffin

clues.clue: Addressed World War II through the backdrop of the Thirty Years' War.
quotes: This German playwright likewise addressed World War II via the backdrop of the Thirty Years War in his play Mother Courage and Her Children.

works.name: The Threepenny Opera
works.description: Brecht's collaboration with Kurt Weill and Elisabeth Hauptmann, based on John Gay's The Beggar's Opera. Famous for the songs 'Mack the Knife' and 'Pirate Jenny.' Frequently clued both as a play and through its individual songs.

clues.clue: Co-written with Kurt Weill; based on The Beggar's Opera.
quotes: This playwright of The Resistible Rise of Arturo Ui worked with longtime collaborator Kurt Weill on an opera based on The Beggar's Opera
This playwright also created The Threepenny Opera with Kurt Weill and Elisabeth Hauptmann.

clues.clue: Features 'Mack the Knife' about 'old Macheath' who wears 'fancy gloves' and keeps a 'jack-knife' 'out of sight.'
quotes: This playwright wrote the lyrics to 'Alabama Song' and a song about 'old Macheath' titled 'Mack the Knife.'
this author described someone 'sneaking round the corner' who wears 'fancy gloves' and keeps a 'jack-knife' 'out of sight.'

clues.clue: The singer of 'Pirate Jenny' is bribed by Polly Peachum to turn Polly's husband over.
quotes: The singer of the song 'Pirate Jenny' is bribed by Polly Peachum to turn Polly's husband over in a play by this author.

clues.clue: Elisabeth Hauptmann is sometimes purported to have written the lion's share of the opera.
quotes: Elisabeth, an author with this surname, is sometimes purported to have written the lion's share of The Threepenny Opera.

clues.clue: Brecht fought bitterly with director G.W. Pabst over the 1931 film adaptation starring Rudolf Forster and Lotte Lenya.
quotes: This author fought bitterly with director G.W. Pabst over the screenplay of a 1931 film adaptation starring Rudolf Forster and Lotte Lenya.

clues.clue: Wole Soyinka adapted it as Opera Wonyosi.
quotes: Wole Soyinka's Opera Wonyosi

clues.clue: An adaptation set in Johannesburg centers on a 'secret secret service' employing Jimmy Long Legs.
quotes: An adaptation of The Threepenny Opera titled for this African city centers on a 'secret secret service' that employs Jimmy Long Legs.

works.name: The Caucasian Chalk Circle
works.description: Inspired by a Chinese zaju gong'an drama by Li Xingdao, this play-within-a-play features the maid Grusha and the judge Azdak. Athol Fugard named his Circle Players after it.

clues.clue: Inspired by a Chinese zaju gong'an drama; adapted a story where a chalk circle is used to identify a child's true mother.
quotes: This author adapted a zaju gong'an drama in a play-within-a-play set during a conflict between two communes.
A gong'an play in which Bao Zheng identifies the mother of a child by placing the child in a chalk circle was adapted into a play by this German Marxist

clues.clue: Grusha refuses to pull the child Michael out of the chalk circle; Azdak makes a Solomonic ruling in Grusha's favor over Natella.
quotes: Grusha refuses to pull Micheal out of the title shape in one work by this author.
A verse drama from what country inspired a play in which Azdak makes a Solomonic ruling in favor of the maid Grusha?

clues.clue: Features a Singer who performs works about 'The Four Generals' and 'The Rotten Bridge.'
quotes: A play by this author features a Singer that performs works about 'The Four Generals' and 'The Rotten Bridge.'

clues.clue: An actor laments 'There must, there must, be some end that would fit'; the play ends 'There must be happy endings, must, must, must!'
quotes: the end of that play features an actor lamenting to the audience 'There must, there must, be some end that would fit.'
A play set in this country ends by declaring, 'There must be happy endings, must, must, must!'

clues.clue: A cup with two bottoms exposes one character as a swindler.
quotes: In one play by this author it is revealed that a cup has two bottoms, exposing one character as a swindler.

clues.clue: Athol Fugard's Circle Players were named for this play.
quotes: Athol Fugard's Circle Players were named for a play by what author

works.name: The Good Person of Szechwan
works.description: A play about Shen Teh, who receives funds from three gods to buy a tobacco shop but must disguise herself as her male cousin Shui Ta to survive. Adapted in Africa as Land of a Million Magicians.

clues.clue: Shen Teh puts on a mask and impersonates her male cousin Shui Ta; sings 'without trampling down twelve others / You cannot help one poor man.'
quotes: a woman puts on a mask and sings in a man's voice, 'without trampling down twelve others / You cannot help one poor man.'
In a play by this author, Shen Teh pretends to be her male cousin Shui Ta to protect herself.

clues.clue: Three gods give Shen Teh funds to buy a tobacco shop; they float away on a cloud at the end, ignoring cries for help.
quotes: That woman stands trial in front of three gods who had earlier given her the funds to buy a tobacco shop.
three gods sing, smile, and wave as they float away on a cloud, ignoring cries for them to stay and help.

clues.clue: A water-carrier goes to Shui Ta to get lodging for some gods.
quotes: Another play by this author opens with a water-carrier who goes to Shui Ta to get lodging for some gods.

clues.clue: Adapted by Mohammed ben Abdallah as Land of a Million Magicians; translated by John Ruganda into Swahili.
quotes: Mohammed ben Abdallah, an author from this country, adapted Brecht's The Good Person of Szechwan into the play Land of a Million Magicians.
John Ruganda translated a play by this author into Swahili for the Nairobi University Players.

clues.clue: Allegorizes the rise of the Nazis; Chicago gangsters expand their cauliflower empire into Cicero, Illinois.
quotes: this author wrote a play in which Chicago gangsters expand their corrupt cauliflower empire into Cicero, Illinois
That play allegorises the rise of the Nazis and is titled The Resistible Rise of Arturo Ui.

quotes: In a play by this author, the protagonist frames Fish for a warehouse fire while working with the Cauliflower Trust.

quotes: The central character of this 'parable play' seduces Ignatius Dullfeet's wife Betty in a scene that parallels Richard III.

quotes: In an epilogue commenting on the death of the title strongman, this playwright wrote 'don't rejoice too soon at your escape- / The womb he crawled from still is going strong.'

clues.clue: At one point attributed to 'Mr Nobody.'

clues.clue: The title character is a lieutenant in the Black Straw Hats who organizes with meat packers against their boss Pierpont Mauler.
quotes: The title character of a play by this author is a lieutenant in a group called Black Straw Hats and organizes with a group of meat packers against their boss.
the meatpacking tycoon Pierpont Mauler becomes disaffected with capitalism after meeting a member of the 'Black Straw Hats'

clues.clue: Revised in collaboration with Charles Laughton after the atomic bombing of Japan.
quotes: In the months after the atomic bombing of Japan, this author created a darker version of one of his plays in collaboration with the actor Charles Laughton.

clues.clue: Galileo's daughter Virginia dies of dysentery in a convent after being unable to find a husband.

works.name: Epic Theatre and the Alienation Effect
works.description: Brecht's central theatrical paradigm, developed partly from studying Chinese theater. The alienation or distancing effect breaks the fourth wall and eliminates Aristotelian catharsis. This concept pervades nearly all Brecht questions as both identifier and standalone topic.

clues.clue: Coined 'alienation effect' / 'distancing effect' / 'estrangement effect' (Verfremdungseffekt) after studying Chinese theater.
quotes: This author drew from the theater of China to theorize his 'distancing effect.'

This author's studies of Chinese acting led him to advocate breaking down the fourth wall between actor and audience, which he called the 'distancing effect,' part of his paradigm of the 'epic theatre.'

clues.clue: Wrote 'A Short Organum for the Theatre' describing epic theatre and the gestus technique.
quotes: This author coined a term translating to 'alienation effect' and wrote 'A Short Organum for the Theatre' to describe 'epic theatre.'
This author explained an acting technique that he called the gestus in his essay 'A Short Organum for the Theater.'

clues.clue: Epic theatre eliminated 'the Aristotelian catharsis, the purging of the emotions through empathy' and created 'untragic heroes' (per Walter Benjamin).
quotes: This genre 'eliminated the Aristotelian catharsis, the purging of the emotions through empathy with the stirring fate of the hero' through the creation of 'untragic heroes' according to an essay by Walter Benjamin.

clues.clue: Essay 'On Chinese Acting' discusses how 'the actor looks at himself' and 'confines himself... to merely quoting the character' as in Mei Lanfang's performances.
quotes: An essay by this author discusses the way that 'the actor looks at himself' and 'confines himself... to merely quoting the character' as in the performances of Mei Lanfang.
An essay by this author credits the lack of a fourth wall in Chinese theater with creating 'alienation effects' he later adapted.

clues.clue: Characters break down the Not/But element by embodying social commentary with the gestus technique.
quotes: Characters in this genre break down the Not / But element by embodying social commentary with the gestus technique.

clues.clue: Actors may change costumes on stage as part of the estrangement effect; the 'non-Aristotelian drama' involves expressing attitudes through gestures.
quotes: Audiences may be forced to view works in this genre as learning tools and not pure entertainment through acts such as changing costumes on stage
This author's 'non-Aristotelian drama' style involves expressing attitudes through gestures.

clues.clue: Developed 'learning-plays' (Lehrstucke) as a didactic subgenre.
quotes: A play by this author titled for a 'lesson on consent' is an example of one of his 'learning-plays.'
Four Communist agents explain their decision to murder a comrade during a Chinese revolution in The Measures Taken, which exemplifies this genre developed by Brecht.

clues.clue: Augusto Boal developed the Theater of the Oppressed as an alternative to Brecht's didacticism.
quotes: Augusto Boal developed the Theater of the Oppressed as an alternative to the didacticism of this author's epic theater
The Theater of the Oppressed was influenced by this earlier theater practitioner's 'learning-plays'

clues.clue: Frederic Jameson claimed Brecht stripped back the 'well-made production' by forcing actors to verbalize alternative choices.
quotes: Frederic Jameson claimed that this author stripped back the 'well-made production' by forcing actors to question their decisions through verbalizing their alternative choices.

clues.clue: The minimalist, artificial staging of Dogville was influenced by Brecht.
quotes: The minimalist, artificial staging of Dogville was influenced by...what German author of The Threepenny Opera?

works.name: Songs and Poems
works.description: Brecht wrote numerous songs and poems that are clued independently, including 'Alabama Song,' 'Mack the Knife,' and several standalone poems about love and politics.

clues.clue: 'Alabama Song': 'show me the way / to the next whiskey bar'; repeats 'I tell you we must die'; recorded by The Doors.
quotes: The Doors recorded a song by this author that begins with a request to 'show me the way / to the next whiskey bar.'
In lyrics written by this author, the singer repeats 'I tell you we must die' while looking for 'the next whiskey bar.'

clues.clue: 'Reminiscence of Marie A.': a poem about a kiss shared under a plum tree, remembered only because a white cloud passed overhead.
quotes: This author wrote a poem reminiscing about a kiss he shared under a plum tree, noting that he would have long since forgotten the kiss if a white cloud hadn't passed overhead at the same moment.

clues.clue: 'Children's Hymn': written in response to Germany's national anthem; includes 'Let us guard and love our home.'
quotes: This man wrote 'Let us guard and love our home, love it as our dearest country as the others love their own' in a poem written in response to his country's national anthem.

clues.clue: 'How Fortunate the Man with None': observes that 'sagacious Solomon,' 'courageous Caesar,' and 'honest Socrates' all met ignominious fates.
quotes: This author...observed that 'sagacious Solomon,' 'courageous Caesar,' and 'honest Socrates' all met ignominious fates in his poem 'How Fortunate the Man with None.'

clues.clue: The 'Fraternization Song' is set on a 'dark May night' with 'boys with mischief in their eyes.'
quotes: This author set a song on a 'dark May night' in which 'boys with mischief in their eyes / will find the girls who fraternise.'

works.name: Other Works and Collaborations
works.description: Various lesser-clued works including In the Jungle of Cities, Mr Puntila and His Man Matti, The Baden-Baden Lesson on Consent, The Measures Taken, the Threepenny Novel, and film work.

clues.clue: In the Jungle of Cities: set in 1912 Chicago; Shlink and Garga engage in a metaphorical boxing match.
quotes: This author wrote, 'the year is 1912; you are in Chicago' in the prologue to a play about Shlink and Garga engaging in a metaphorical boxing match.
Garga and the lumber merchant Shlink metaphorically spar in a Brecht play titled for being in the jungle of one of these places.

clues.clue: The Baden-Baden Lesson on Consent caused a riot when the clown Smith is dismembered by friends to ease his pain.
quotes: A play by this author caused a riot after a scene in which the clown Smith is dismembered by his friends to ease his pain.

clues.clue: The Measures Taken: four Communist agents explain murdering a comrade during a Chinese revolution.
quotes: Four Communist agents explain their decision to murder a comrade during a Chinese revolution in The Measures Taken

clues.clue: Threepenny Novel: the one-legged Fewkoombey murders William Coax with his prosthetic leg; falsely convicted for drowning Mary Sawyer.
quotes: a novel where the one-legged George Fewkoombey murders William Coax with his own prosthetic leg before being falsely convicted and hanged for the drowning of Mary Sawyer

clues.clue: Mr Puntila and His Man Matti: set in Finland; chauffeur Matti holds up landowner's plan to marry daughter Eva to a diplomat.
quotes: Building off her translation of this country's play The Sawdust Princess, Margarete Steffin teamed with Brecht on a comedy set in this country, in which the chauffeur Matti holds up a landowner's plan to marry his daughter Eva off to a diplomat.

clues.clue: Suggested the idea for The Cradle Will Rock by Marc Blitzstein and was its dedicatee.
quotes: This author suggested the idea for, and was the dedicatee of, a musical about Steeltown U.S.A.; that musical by Marc Blitzstein is The Cradle Will Rock.

clues.clue: Only Hollywood screenplay was Hangmen Also Die, directed by a fellow German expat.
quotes: This author's only Hollywood screenplay was for Hangmen Also Die, which was directed by a fellow expat from the same country as this author.

clues.clue: Wrote slapstick film Mysteries of a Barbershop starring Karl Valentin, a major influence on Brecht.
quotes: This author wrote the slapstick film Mysteries of a Barbershop starring Karl Valentin, who was one of the main influences on this writer.

clues.clue: His daughter recites one of his poems at the beginning of the film Germany, Pale Mother.
quotes: This author's daughter recites one of his poems at the beginning of a film titled for that poem, in which Anna's mother has all her teeth removed to prevent her facial paralysis from spreading.

cards.front: Playwright: essay 'On Chinese Acting' discusses how 'the actor looks at himself' and 'confines himself to merely quoting the character' as in Mei Lanfang's performances
cards.work: Epic Theatre and the Alienation Effect

cards.front: Playwright: Augusto Boal developed the Theater of the Oppressed as an alternative to this author's didactic epic theater
cards.work: Epic Theatre and the Alienation Effect

cards.front: Playwright: Frederic Jameson claimed this author stripped back the 'well-made production' by forcing actors to verbalize alternative choices
cards.work: Epic Theatre and the Alienation Effect

cards.front: Concept: Walter Benjamin wrote that this theatrical genre 'eliminated the Aristotelian catharsis' through 'untragic heroes'
cards.work: Epic Theatre and the Alienation Effect

cards.front: Concept: characters break down the 'Not/But' element by embodying social commentary with the gestus technique in this theatrical genre
cards.work: Epic Theatre and the Alienation Effect

cards.front: Playwright: the minimalist, artificial staging of Dogville was influenced by this author
cards.work: Epic Theatre and the Alienation Effect

cards.front: Poet: 'Alabama Song' begins 'show me the way / to the next whiskey bar' and was later recorded by The Doors
Poet: 'Reminiscence of Marie A.' describes a kiss under a plum tree remembered only because a white cloud passed overhead
Poet: 'Children's Hymn' was written as a response to Germany's national anthem
Poet: 'How Fortunate the Man with None' observes that 'sagacious Solomon,' 'courageous Caesar,' and 'honest Socrates' all met ignominious fates
Play: set in 1912 Chicago, Shlink and Garga engage in a metaphorical boxing match
cards.back: In the Jungle of Cities (Bertolt Brecht)

cards.front: Play: caused a riot when the clown Smith is dismembered by friends to ease his pain; an example of 'learning-plays'
cards.back: The Baden-Baden Lesson on Consent (Bertolt Brecht)

cards.front: Play: four Communist agents explain their decision to murder a comrade during a Chinese revolution
Novel: the one-legged George Fewkoombey murders William Coax with his prosthetic leg; falsely convicted for drowning Mary Sawyer
Playwright: suggested the idea for The Cradle Will Rock by Marc Blitzstein and was its dedicatee
Playwright: only Hollywood screenplay was Hangmen Also Die, directed by a fellow German expat
Playwright: wrote slapstick film Mysteries of a Barbershop starring Karl Valentin, a major influence
Play: set in Finland, the chauffeur Matti holds up a landowner's plan to marry his daughter Eva to a diplomat; based on The Sawdust Princess
cards.back: Mr Puntila and His Man Matti (Bertolt Brecht)

summary: Bertolt Brecht was a German playwright and the leading practitioner of 'epic theatre,' a theatrical paradigm he developed in opposition to Aristotelian drama.
